region_of_origin: United Kingdom (widely consumed in Singapore and Southeast Asia)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general malted milk beverage class; no specific Horlicks flavoured variant node exists. FOODON_03301521 is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers non-alcoholic malted beverages broadly; no flavoured Horlicks-specific code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for flavoured Horlicks instant drink mix; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

preparation_method: dissolved in hot or cold water or milk

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 200 g (one cup) of Flavoured Horlicks prepared by dissolving the powder in hot or cold water, as commonly consumed in Singapore households and kopitiams.

nutrient_source.title: Horlicks (Flavoured) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Foodo database entry for Flavoured Horlicks (any type), cr_id D04020006. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell, Holt, Brand-Miller; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Flavoured Horlicks (any type) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 55 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: plain Horlicks malted milk drink powder prepared with water, which is reported at approximately GI 55 (medium) in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Foster-Powell et al. (2008). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (22 g per 200 g serving) / 100 = 12.1. The flavoured variant contains added sugar (5.85 g/100 g) which may marginally elevate GI; the estimate of 55 is therefore conservative. Note: GI 55 is considered 'low' by the University of Sydney GI Database classification (GI <= 55 is low).

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (5.85 g/100 g) and low dietary fibre (0.65 g/100 g); sodium is relatively low at 66 mg/100 g but sugar-sweetened beverages independently elevate blood pressure per AHA 2018 guidelines.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Flavoured Horlicks because its added sugar content (5.85 g/100 g) independently raises blood pressure risk; if a malted drink is desired, choose a plain, no-added-sugar variant and prepare with low-fat milk to benefit from potassium without the sugar load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) classification; added sugar (5.85 g/100 g), low GI (~55), and liquid form accelerates glucose absorption, raising postprandial blood glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Flavoured Horlicks is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage and is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the ADA recommends avoiding SSBs entirely, and even 'less sugar' Horlicks variants retain added sugar — plain unsweetened malt beverages or water are preferred alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar content (5.85 g/100 g) in a liquid form contributes to elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk; low saturated fat data unavailable but overall fat is low (0.5 g/100 g).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Flavoured Horlicks should be consumed sparingly or avoided due to its added sugar content, which is associated with elevated triglycerides and increased CVD risk per AHA dietary guidelines; the calcium (287 mg/100 g) and iron (1.55 mg/100 g) fortification offer minor benefits that do not outweigh the sugar risk in the flavoured form.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (5.85 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory pathways; absence of omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenols, or cruciferous compounds means no anti-inflammatory benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Flavoured Horlicks because its added sugar content promotes systemic inflammation; the calcium content (287 mg/100 g) is beneficial for patients on corticosteroids but is better obtained from plain low-fat milk or fortified unsweetened plant milk without the sugar burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flavoured Horlicks (any type) is an ultra-processed instant malted milk drink powder manufactured by Horlicks (a brand historically associated with GlaxoSmithKline and now Unilever in some markets), widely consumed across Singapore, Malaysia, and South Asia. The product is prepared by dissolving the powder in hot or cold water or milk, producing a warm or chilled malted beverage with a sweet, malty flavour profile. The 'flavoured' designation covers variants such as chocolate, vanilla, and other added-flavour formulations, all of which contain added sugar in addition to the base malt and milk solids. Per 100 g of powder, the product provides 56 kcal of energy, 1.45 g protein, 0.5 g fat, 11.0 g carbohydrate (of which 5.85 g is sugar), 0.65 g dietary fibre, 66 mg sodium, 287 mg calcium, and 1.55 mg iron. The high calcium content reflects the product's fortification profile, which is marketed as a nutritional supplement for children and adults. Clinically, Flavoured Horlicks is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension due to its added sugar content and liquid delivery form, which accelerates glucose absorption. The low glycemic index (~55) and moderate glycemic load per serving (~12.1) still warrant caution in glycaemic management contexts due to the added sugar. The product is vegetarian and halal-certified in Singapore but contains gluten (wheat, barley) and milk, making it unsuitable for those with coeliac disease or lactose intolerance. In Singapore, Horlicks is commonly consumed as a breakfast or supper drink in kopitiams and at home, often ordered as 'Horlicks panas' (hot Horlicks) or 'Horlicks ais' (iced Horlicks).
common_user_queries: Can I drink Horlicks if I have high blood pressure?
What is the sugar content of Horlicks?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D04020006) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved source (University of Sydney GI Database, plain Horlicks) and clearly documented as an analogous estimate.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, cholesterol, full vitamin panel, and moisture data for Flavoured Horlicks are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry (D04020006) or in USDA FoodData Central for the closest equivalent malted milk drink powder as of 2026-04-16. 2. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on plain Horlicks malted milk drink powder from the University of Sydney GI Database; the flavoured variant with added sugar may have a marginally higher GI. Note: GI 55 is classified as 'low' by the University of Sydney GI Database. 3. The 'flavoured' designation covers multiple Horlicks variants (chocolate, vanilla, etc.); nutrient values represent the HPB-reported average across types. 4. Dietary tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from the input are retained as contextual tags only; these do not override the SSB classification for clinical health assessments. 5. No zero-value core macros are present; the zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' to align with allowed values.

Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' as GI 55 falls within the 'low' category (GI <= 55) according to the University of Sydney GI Database classification, and updated the gi_source_match_notes to reflect this.
